ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Orange Chicken or Chinese-American battered fried chicken with sweet orange glaze. Mapped to broader 'chicken dish' term (FOODON_03302051) as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chinese-American orange chicken. A00GJ (Chicken meat, cooked) used as the closest defensible broader anchor. The battered, deep-fried, and glazed preparation is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Orange Chicken or Chinese-American fried chicken dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant or fast-casual serving of Orange Chicken is approximately 250 g, providing around 788 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 48.8 g fat, and 56.3 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter; and USDA SR Legacy — Sweet and sour sauce, restaurant-prepared
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Chinese-American Orange Chicken as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for battered fried chicken (FDC ID 171477) and sweet-and-sour style sauce components, cross-referenced with the input enriched_json values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9). Input values are within plausible range for this dish type and have been retained where consistent with USDA component data. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g based on USDA fried chicken data. Cholesterol estimated at approximately 65 mg per 100 g. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not available at composite dish level.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken with sweet sauce
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chinese-American Orange Chicken. GI estimated at 72 (high) based on analogous foods: battered fried chicken with starchy coating (GI ~65–70) combined with a high-sugar sweet orange glaze (GI contribution elevating composite to ~70–75). Reference: Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 56.25) / 100 = 40.5 g per 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100 g; ~1050 mg per 250 g serving) from soy sauce and seasoning, combined with high caloric density from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Orange Chicken due to its high sodium content (~1050 mg per serving, representing approximately 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit), which can elevate blood pressure; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) and high sugar content (10.5 g per 100 g; ~26 g per serving) from the sweet orange glaze, combined with a starchy batter coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Orange Chicken due to its high GI (~72) and high sugar load (~26 g per serving), which can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose spikes; if consumed, it should be in very small portions as an occasional treat alongside low-GI foods and with close blood glucose monitoring.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (19.5 g per 100 g) including saturated fat (~4.5 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, high sodium, and high caloric density contributing to dyslipidaemia and weight gain risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Orange Chicken due to its high saturated fat (~11.3 g per serving), high sodium (~1050 mg per serving), and high caloric density (~788 kcal per serving), all of which can adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and body weight; consumption should be infrequent and portion-controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High refined sugar and deep-fried fat content may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Orange Chicken as its high refined sugar and deep-fried fat content may exacerbate pro-inflammatory pathways; occasional small portions are preferable to regular consumption.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Orange Chicken (陈皮鸡 / 橙汁鸡, Chénpí jī) is a signature dish of Chinese-American fast-casual cuisine, most famously associated with the Panda Express restaurant chain in North America. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of boneless chicken thigh (dark meat) that are coated in a seasoned wheat-flour and cornstarch batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, and then tossed in a thick, glossy, sweet-tangy orange-flavored glaze. The sauce is typically made from orange juice or orange extract, sugar, rice vinegar, soy sauce, fresh ginger, garlic, and dried red chili flakes, producing a bright citrus aroma with a predominantly sweet flavor profile and mild heat. The texture contrast between the crispy battered exterior and the tender, juicy chicken interior is a defining characteristic. Nutritionally, Orange Chicken is energy-dense at approximately 315 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (11.5 g), high fat (19.5 g, including ~4.5 g saturated fat from deep-frying), and significant carbohydrate (22.5 g) and sugar (10.5 g) from the glaze. Sodium is high at 420 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals managing blood glucose. A typical 250 g serving provides approximately 788 kcal, 28.8 g protein, 48.8 g fat, 56.3 g carbohydrate, and ~1050 mg sodium. Clinically, Orange Chicken is a high-concern food for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its high sodium, high sugar, high GI, and high saturated fat content. It is best consumed as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary component. The dish is distinct from the traditional Hunanese dried orange peel chicken (陈皮鸡), which uses bitter dried citrus peel, is much less sweet, and is significantly spicier.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia) contains a direct entry for Chinese-American Orange Chicken as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central battered fried chicken entries and sweet sauce component data, cross-referenced with the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.9). The input values are within plausible range for this dish type. Saturated fat and cholesterol are estimated from USDA component data. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for Chinese-American Orange Chicken as a composite dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or other approved sources as of April 2026. 2. Saturated fat (4.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (65 mg per 100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for battered fried chicken (FDC ID 171477) and are not directly reported for this composite dish. 3. GI value of 72 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Orange Chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed GI tables as of April 2026. 4. The input source cited ('Public Health Nutrition Journal', Cambridge) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central is used instead. 5. Halal status is listed as false (unverified) as commercial preparations vary by restaurant and supplier. 6. Portions deduplicated: input contained two identical 250 g serving entries; one retained. 7. This dish is a Chinese-American creation and does not require Malay, Tamil, or Hindi multilingual coverage under Project Veritas v2 rules (those are mandatory only for Singaporean dishes).
